one_line_takeaway: SafeFQL adds an HJ-reachability-style safety critic and a strict feasibility-gated (not additively weighted) actor objective on top of one-step Flow Q-Learning, letting an offline safe-RL policy hit near-zero constraint violations with a single forward pass, avoiding the rejection sampling that diffusion/flow safety baselines like FISOR and SafeIFQL require at inference.
key_finding: Across the Boat Navigation task, SafeFQL achieves zero safety violations across all 500 evaluated episodes while attaining the highest reward among the compared methods. Across the five Safety Gymnasium Safe Velocity MuJoCo tasks (Hopper, HalfCheetah, Ant, Walker2D, Swimmer), SafeFQL achieves the lowest cost (fewest violations) among all baselines while attaining the highest reward among frameworks with comparably low cost (Figure 2, mean +/- std over 5 seeds). On action-sampling efficiency (Boat Navigation), SafeFQL reaches its safety rate ceiling with a single action sample (N=1), whereas the diffusion/flow rejection-sampling baselines FISOR and SafeIFQL need up to N=16 candidate samples to approach comparable safety rates (Figure 3). On compute cost (Figure 4, 5 trial runs): training time was 113s for FISOR, 64s for SafeIFQL, and 128s for SafeFQL (SafeFQL costs modestly more to train); per-episode inference time was 0.24-0.34s for FISOR (N=1 to N=16) and 0.21-0.35s for SafeIFQL (N=1 to N=16), versus 0.14s for SafeFQL at N=1 -- reported in the conclusion as a 2.5x inference-time speedup over the diffusion/flow rejection-sampling baselines.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Safe Flow Q-Learning (SafeFQL), an offline safe reinforcement learning method that extends Flow Q-Learning (FQL, Park et al. 2025) to constrained/safety-critical settings. It combines (1) a Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability-inspired safety critic trained via a max-backup Bellman recursion (yc = max{l(x), gamma*Vc(x')}), which yields a state-wise worst-case feasibility estimate rather than an expected-cost penalty; (2) a flow-matching behavior-policy 'teacher' trained by behavioral cloning and distilled into a deterministic one-step 'student' actor, avoiding iterative denoising/rejection sampling at deployment; and (3) a feasibility-gated actor objective that uses a binary gate zeta = I{Qc(x, a_omega) < 0} to enforce strict priority: when the actor's action is predicted infeasible, the update ignores reward and only minimizes the safety critic; only once the action is predicted feasible does the update switch to reward maximization. This is explicitly contrasted with a naive additive Lagrangian objective (Eq. 14), which the authors argue suffers from reward/safety gradients trading off near the feasibility boundary.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a control-theoretic/offline-RL safety paper (avoiding constraint violations such as collisions or exceeding a velocity threshold during control), not an adversarial-security paper. There is no attacker, no adversarial perturbation, no model-extraction/poisoning/jailbreak framing, and no security threat model of any kind is defined or assumed in the text.
limitations: The paper is offline-only and makes no online-training safety claims (explicitly noted in the cover-page contribution list). Reported latency/compute gains are specific to the evaluated implementations, hardware, and benchmark settings, and the empirical safety/reward findings are stated to be established only on the reported benchmarks and 'may vary across datasets and task distributions' (cover page). The authors themselves flag that the hard binary indicator gate zeta used in the feasibility-gated actor loss (Eq. 15/16) could in principle produce a non-smooth loss landscape and could impact training stability; they suggest continuous masking functions or soft Lagrangian relaxations as future refinements but note these would require additional hyperparameter tuning, which they consider undesirable. The safety-critic value function still depends on the reachability/backup formulation being an accurate approximation from a finite offline dataset (no formal proof of the safety guarantee is claimed on held-out/unseen states, only empirical near-zero-violation behavior on the tested environments).

datasets_used: Custom Safe Boat Navigation dataset: 2,500 initial states x 400-timestep rollouts, uniform-random action collection, dataset size 1M (Boat Navigation config in Table 2)
DSRL offline dataset (Liu et al. 2024) for Safety Gymnasium Safe Velocity MuJoCo tasks: Hopper 1.32M transitions, HalfCheetah 249K, Ant 2.09M, Walker2D 2.12M, Swimmer 1.68M
quantitative_results: Boat Navigation: SafeFQL attains zero safety violations across all 500 evaluated episodes while achieving the highest reward among compared methods
Safety Gymnasium (5 MuJoCo Safe Velocity tasks): SafeFQL achieves the lowest cost among all baselines and the highest reward among frameworks with comparably low cost (Fig. 2, mean +/- std, 5 seeds)

Action-sampling efficiency (Boat Navigation): SafeFQL reaches its top safety rate at N=1 action sample; FISOR and SafeIFQL require up to N=16 rejection-sampling candidates to approach comparable safety rates (Fig. 3)
Training compute time (mean over 5 runs, same hardware): FISOR 113s, SafeIFQL 64s, SafeFQL (Ours) 128s (Fig. 4)
Inference time per episode (mean over 5 runs): FISOR 0.24s (N=1) to 0.34s (N=8)/0.33s (N=16); SafeIFQL 0.21s (N=1) to 0.35s (N=4,8,16); SafeFQL (Ours) 0.14s at N=1
Conclusion states SafeFQL achieves an inference-time speedup of 2.5x over the diffusion/flow rejection-sampling baselines
baselines_compared: BEAR-Lag (Lagrangian-dual version of Kumar et al. 2019)
FISOR (Zheng et al. 2024) -- diffusion-based, evaluated with rejection sampling (R.S.)
SafeIFQL -- the paper's own 'Safe Flow Matching version of IQL (Kostrikov et al. 2022)' baseline, evaluated with rejection sampling (R.S.)
